_description: Scene-level script. Contains all dialogue, narration, sound, and pacing for each scene as an ordered sequence of beats. This is the narrative writing layer — no panel layout decisions are made here.

scenes.title: The Defiant Toaster

emotional_arc.turning_point: The screw resists — the toaster wins this round
emotional_arc.arc_note: Adèle's fixation on the toaster is a proxy for control over her post-breakup life. The harder the screw resists, the more she needs it to turn.

beats.description: Close-up of Adèle's hands pressing a butter knife flat against a Phillips-head screw on the side panel of a chrome toaster. The metal slips. She adjusts her grip.

beats.text: Tuesday. 9:47 PM. The toaster had been broken since March.
Probably a Phillips head. Most likely a number two. The butter knife was... statistically adequate.
SKREEE
beats.description: Metal butter knife scraping against chrome screw head, slipping
Adèle pushes her glasses up with the back of her wrist. A thin scratch now runs across the toaster's side panel. She stares at it like it personally insulted her.
The apartment hums. The drafty window rattles faintly. Adèle and the toaster regard each other.
beats.purpose: Establishing the quiet, lonely rhythm of her evenings

scenes.pacing_notes: Slow and deliberate. Each beat should feel like a small eternity — this is a woman who has nothing but time and a broken appliance.
scenes.scene_thesis: Control is Adèle's love language, and the toaster is her current relationship.
scenes.transition_out: The silence is about to be shattered.

scenes.title: A Neon Knock

emotional_arc.turning_point: Adèle opens the door and sees the flamingo
emotional_arc.arc_note: The knock is Thomas's signature move — loud, unannounced, impossible to ignore. The flamingo is the exclamation mark.

beats.text: BAM BAM BAM
beats.description: Three aggressive knocks on the apartment door, rattling the hinges
Adèle flinches. The butter knife clatters onto the table. She stares at the door like it just detonated.
Adèle opens the door. Thomas is leaning against the doorframe with one arm. Under the other arm, tucked casually like a baguette, is a four-foot neon-green flamingo.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Hey. So — quick thing. Completely unrelated to anything. Can I use your bathtub?
beats.delivery: Rapid, casual, as if asking to borrow sugar
beats.subtext: He needs shelter and is using absurdity to disarm her

beats.description: Adèle stares at the flamingo. The flamingo stares at Adèle. Thomas smiles.
beats.purpose: Comic beat — the sheer absurdity needs a moment to land

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: Thomas... what is that.
beats.delivery: Flat. Not a question. A diagnosis.
beats.subtext: She already knows this is going to ruin her evening and she doesn't have the energy to stop it

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: It's a flamingo. Obviously. The colour is — look, that's a whole separate conversation.
beats.delivery: Interrupting himself, deflecting the obvious follow-up
beats.subtext: He doesn't want to explain the green part yet

scenes.pacing_notes: Sharp contrast with Scene 1. The knock should feel like a grenade. Then the reveal of the flamingo slows everything down for the comedy.
scenes.scene_thesis: Thomas arrives the way he always does: loudly, absurdly, and with something that cannot be ignored.
scenes.transition_out: Before Adèle can object, Thomas is already moving toward the bathroom.

scenes.title: The Tub Negotiation

emotional_arc.turning_point: Adèle realizes arguing is more exhausting than agreeing
emotional_arc.arc_note: This is a negotiation Adèle was always going to lose — not because Thomas is more persuasive, but because she's too tired to fight.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: My plumbing is — you don't want details. Trust me. The point is, she needs water. Shallow water. Your tub is perfect.
beats.delivery: Fast, steamrolling, gesturing at the bathtub like a real estate agent
beats.subtext: The plumbing excuse is probably a lie, but the need is real

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: She.
beats.delivery: One word. Flat. Processing.
beats.subtext: She just caught the pronoun and is horrified

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Her name is Giselle. Which — I didn't pick. She came pre-named.
beats.delivery: Defensive but proud, like introducing a girlfriend
beats.subtext: He absolutely did pick the name

beats.text: It's 10 PM on a Tuesday. There is a green bird in my hallway. This is my life now.
beats.tone: Flat resignation, bordering on dark comedy

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: ...bathroom's on the left.
beats.delivery: Trailing off, already turning away to the kitchen
beats.subtext: She surrenders not because she agrees but because resistance costs more energy than she has

beats.description: Thomas grins and marches past her carrying the flamingo under his arm. Adèle closes her eyes for a full second, then walks back to her toaster.
beats.emotional_state: Defeated but too tired to care

scenes.pacing_notes: Quick dialogue volleys, then a hard stop on Adèle's internal monologue. The scene should feel like a tennis match that ends when one player just walks off the court.
scenes.scene_thesis: Adèle's exhaustion is Thomas's greatest weapon.
scenes.transition_out: Ten minutes of quiet. Then: clicking.

scenes.title: Corn and Clicking

emotional_arc.turning_point: Adèle sees the corn — the situation is more elaborate than she assumed
emotional_arc.arc_note: The clicking sound is Adèle's curiosity winning over her desire to be left alone.

beats.text: tik tik tik tik
beats.description: Dry corn kernels tapping against porcelain bathtub, quiet and rhythmic

beats.text: That's not a plumbing sound.

beats.description: Adèle stands in the bathroom doorway. Thomas is sitting on the edge of the tub, dropping dried corn kernels one by one into a pale green palm. The flamingo pecks delicately from his hand.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: You brought... food for it.
beats.delivery: Slow, each word chosen carefully, as if describing evidence at a crime scene
beats.subtext: The corn means this wasn't spontaneous — he planned this

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Her. And yeah, she likes the dry kind better. The wet stuff makes her — anyway. It's a texture thing.
beats.delivery: Casual expertise, interrupted mid-sentence by self-awareness
beats.subtext: He knows way too much about this bird for someone who just 'found' it

beats.description: The flamingo tilts its head and looks directly at Adèle. Its eye is a small black marble, bright and oddly intelligent.
beats.emotional_state: A crack in her defenses — genuine curiosity

scenes.pacing_notes: Start with just sound, then reveal. The corn changes everything — it proves intent.
scenes.scene_thesis: Thomas didn't stumble into this situation. He chose it. That's what unsettles Adèle.
scenes.transition_out: The bird blinks. And Adèle's world tilts.

scenes.title: Real, Live, and Neon-Green

emotional_arc.turning_point: The bird makes a sound. It IS real.
emotional_arc.arc_note: Until this moment, Adèle could have told herself it was a lawn ornament. The sound kills that fiction.

beats.text: krrr
beats.description: A soft, throaty sound from the flamingo — a quiet gurgling chirr
Adèle's eyes widen. The flamingo turns its head ninety degrees and blinks at her. A slow, deliberate, very-much-alive blink.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: Thomas. That's... that is a real bird.
beats.delivery: Whispered. As if saying it louder would make it more true.
beats.subtext: Her analytical brain is crashing — this does not compute

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Bankrupt roadside zoo. Outside Valence. She was the last one left. They were just... going to leave her.
beats.delivery: Quieter than usual, genuine for once, not performing
beats.subtext: He sees himself in the abandoned bird

beats.description: Neither of them says anything. The flamingo shifts its weight from one leg to the other. The bathroom faucet drips once.
beats.purpose: The absurdity lands. Something real has entered the conversation.

scenes.pacing_notes: This scene needs air. Let the revelation breathe. Thomas's one genuine moment shouldn't be rushed.
scenes.scene_thesis: When Thomas stops performing, the truth hits harder.
scenes.transition_out: But the serious moment can't last. Thomas has a landlord problem.

scenes.title: The Landlord's 'Parrot'

emotional_arc.opening_state: The gravity of the situation — landlord consequences
emotional_arc.turning_point: The 'parrot' excuse is so absurd it breaks Adèle
emotional_arc.arc_note: This is the emotional release. Adèle stops fighting the absurdity and just... sits down.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Okay, one thing. Mrs. Gable absolutely cannot find out about this.
beats.delivery: Leaning forward, conspiratorial, index finger raised
beats.subtext: He's already been caught and is managing damage

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: Thomas. It's four feet tall.
beats.delivery: Deadpan. Stating objective reality.
beats.subtext: There is no possible cover story

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: I told her it was a large parrot.
beats.delivery: Completely straight-faced. No irony. He means it.
beats.subtext: He genuinely believes this will work

beats.description: A beat. Adèle's mouth opens. Nothing comes out.
beats.purpose: The joke needs a fraction of a second to land

beats.description: Adèle slides down the wall until she's sitting on the bathmat. Her shoulders start shaking. She's laughing — really laughing — for the first time in weeks. She tilts her head back against the tiles.
beats.emotional_state: Uncontrolled laughter, releasing weeks of tension

beats.text: She hadn't laughed like that since before Marc.
beats.tone: Quiet, understated. Not sentimental — just a fact.

scenes.pacing_notes: The 'parrot' line is the punchline of the chapter. Give it room. Adèle's laughter is the emotional climax of Act 1.
scenes.scene_thesis: Sometimes the best medicine is something too stupid to argue with.
scenes.transition_out: They settle in. The night is no longer about the toaster.

scenes.title: Pizza on the Bathmat

emotional_arc.opening_state: Warm, shared intimacy — siblings on the floor
emotional_arc.turning_point: The cold pizza triggers a childhood memory
emotional_arc.arc_note: The bathroom floor becomes their childhood bedroom. Same positions, same food, different decade.

beats.description: Wide overhead shot: Adèle and Thomas sit on opposite sides of a grease-stained pizza box on the bathroom floor. The flamingo stands in the bathtub behind them, one leg folded.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: This is exactly like Lyon. Floor pizza. Terrible lighting. Except the bird is new.
beats.delivery: Mouth half-full, waving a pizza slice for emphasis
beats.subtext: He's deliberately invoking their shared history to deepen the connection

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: The lighting in Lyon was worse. You had that... that lamp shaped like a football.
beats.delivery: Trailing off into a half-smile, remembering
beats.subtext: She's letting the memory in without resistance

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: That lamp was a design classic.
beats.delivery: Mock-offended, pointing with the pizza crust
beats.subtext: They both know it was hideous

beats.text: Same positions. Same cold pizza. He still eats the crust first.

beats.description: They chew. The flamingo shifts. The radiator hisses. Nothing needs to be said.
beats.purpose: The warmth of the scene is in the silence, not the words

scenes.pacing_notes: Let this scene be slow and warm. It's the emotional center of the story. The dialogue should feel like two people who know each other's rhythms by heart.
scenes.scene_thesis: Some bonds are measured in cold pizza on floors.
scenes.transition_out: The memories start flowing — and Thomas goes first.

scenes.title: The Haircut Incident

emotional_arc.turning_point: Adèle's dry rebuttal — it was his fault
emotional_arc.arc_note: The haircut story is their greatest hit — a well-worn anecdote both of them have told a hundred times. Each has their own version.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Remember the kitchen shears? I wore a beanie for a MONTH because of you.
beats.delivery: Dramatic, pointing at her face, grinning
beats.subtext: He loves this story — it makes him the victim and that's his favourite role

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: You moved. I told you not to move. Statistically, one hundred percent your fault.
beats.delivery: Dry, matter-of-fact, no remorse whatsoever
beats.subtext: She has rehearsed this defense many times

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: The beanie was bright red. I looked like a — like a postbox with legs.
beats.delivery: Laughing at his own description, changing the subject mid-thought
beats.subtext: He actually liked the beanie

beats.description: Thomas tugs at his green beanie. Adèle raises an eyebrow. The implication lands: he's still wearing a beanie.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: ...you lost the red one in two weeks.
beats.delivery: Trailing off, quiet smile, looking at the green one
beats.subtext: She notices the pattern — he's always covering something

scenes.pacing_notes: Fast and playful. The dialogue should bounce like a ping-pong match. The beanie callback is a gentle visual punchline.
scenes.scene_thesis: Their best arguments are the ones neither of them wants to win.
scenes.transition_out: The memories keep coming — deeper now.

scenes.title: The Stairwell Sofa and Haunted Radiator

emotional_arc.turning_point: Thomas reveals the pennies — still in his pocket
emotional_arc.closing_state: Bittersweet recognition of who they are
emotional_arc.arc_note: The pennies are a physical artifact of their childhood. Thomas keeping them says more than any dialogue could.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: And the sofa. On the stairs. Nobody could get past for three days.
beats.delivery: Building momentum, gesturing with both hands
beats.subtext: He's proud of the chaos they caused

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: Madame Fournier had to use the service elevator. She called us ghost children.
beats.delivery: Half-laughing, remembering the old woman's fury
beats.subtext: She loved being invisible and powerful at the same time

beats.description: Thomas reaches into his jacket pocket and pulls out three copper pennies. He holds them in his open palm under the warm glow of the bathroom light.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: The radiator offerings. Still got 'em.
beats.delivery: Quiet. No joke. Just a fact.
beats.subtext: He carries their childhood with him literally

beats.text: He bought candy with those pennies. Split it exactly in half. Even the broken pieces.
beats.tone: Fond, specific detail that reveals deep memory

beats.description: They look at the pennies. The radiator in the bathroom clanks once, as if on cue.
beats.purpose: The coincidence of the radiator sound connects past and present

scenes.pacing_notes: Start fast with the sofa story, then slow way down for the pennies reveal. The radiator sound at the end should feel like the apartment is listening.
scenes.scene_thesis: Thomas hoards memories in his pockets. Adèle hoards them in her head. Same instinct, different filing systems.
scenes.transition_out: The night shifts — from nostalgia to play.

scenes.title: The Microwave Mimic

emotional_arc.turning_point: The flamingo actually mimics the beep
emotional_arc.closing_state: Triumphant laughter, interrupted by a furious dog
emotional_arc.arc_note: This is the high point of pure fun — two adults teaching a bird a sound. The consequences are immediate and hilarious.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Okay okay okay. Watch this. Ready?
beats.delivery: Buzzing with energy, crouching next to the microwave
beats.subtext: He lives for these moments of pointless invention

beats.text: BEEP BEEP BEEP
beats.description: Thomas mimicking the microwave timer sound with his mouth, surprisingly accurate

beats.text: BRREEP
beats.description: The flamingo's attempt at the microwave beep — a throaty, warbling approximation
Thomas and Adèle freeze. They look at each other. The flamingo did it. It actually did it.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: That's... that is statistically improbable.
beats.delivery: Barely containing laughter, adjusting her glasses
beats.subtext: She's genuinely delighted and it shows — a rare breach in her composure

beats.text: BRRREEP BRRREEP BRRREEP
beats.description: The flamingo going off, repeating the sound on a loop

beats.text: WOOF WOOF WOOF WOOF
beats.description: Napoleon, the neighbor's dog, erupting into frenzied barking through the wall

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Napoleon.
beats.delivery: One word. Instant regret.
beats.subtext: He forgot about the dog

scenes.pacing_notes: Build the anticipation, then rapid-fire escalation. The flamingo's first beep is the peak — everything after is controlled chaos.
scenes.scene_thesis: Joy is contagious, destructive, and makes terrible neighbors.
scenes.transition_out: The night catches up with them. Time to go.

scenes.title: 2:00 AM Departure

emotional_arc.opening_state: Warm fatigue, the night winding down
emotional_arc.turning_point: Thomas actually leaves — the door opens to cold air
emotional_arc.closing_state: Sudden vacuum of energy and silence
emotional_arc.arc_note: Thomas leaves the way he came — quickly, without ceremony. The silence he leaves behind is the real goodbye.

beats.description: Thomas scoops up the flamingo with practiced ease, tucking it under one arm. He grabs the empty pizza box with the other hand.
beats.emotional_state: Content but restless — ready to move again

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_B
beats.text: Right. Early start tomorrow. Big plans. Very legitimate plans.
beats.delivery: Rapid, already halfway to the door, not looking back
beats.subtext: He has no plans. He doesn't know where he's sleeping.

beats.speaker: CHARACTER_A
beats.text: Thomas...
beats.delivery: Trailing off. She doesn't know what she wants to say.
beats.subtext: Stay. Be careful. Come back. All of it.

beats.description: Thomas pauses in the open doorway. The hallway light behind him turns his silhouette — and the flamingo's — into a single, absurd, backlit shape. He offers a small, sincere smile. No joke.

beats.text: click
beats.description: The front door closing gently
The apartment is suddenly, completely, oppressively quiet. The hallway light that was leaking under the door fades as Thomas walks away.
beats.purpose: The absence is physical. Thomas takes the sound with him.

scenes.pacing_notes: Quick exit, then a long, held silence. The door closing should feel final. The silence after should linger across multiple panels.
scenes.scene_thesis: Thomas always leaves the way he arrives: without warning. The difference is what he leaves behind.
scenes.transition_out: Adèle is alone. But something is different.

scenes.title: Corn in the Cabinet

emotional_arc.opening_state: Alone in the quiet, the broken toaster still waiting
emotional_arc.turning_point: Instead of fixing the toaster, she opens the cabinet
emotional_arc.closing_state: Quiet acceptance — a choice made without words
emotional_arc.arc_note: The corn is the opposite of the toaster. The toaster is about control. The corn is about invitation. Adèle chooses the corn.

beats.description: Adèle stands in her kitchen. The broken toaster is still on the table, the butter knife beside it. She looks at it.
She doesn't pick up the butter knife. Instead, she reaches into a grocery bag on the counter and pulls out a small plastic bag of dried yellow corn kernels.

beats.text: Just in case.
beats.tone: Simple. No analysis. No qualifiers. For once.

beats.description: She opens the wooden kitchen cabinet and places the bag of corn on the middle shelf, next to her colour-coded spice jars. She adjusts it so it sits straight. Then she closes the cabinet door.
beats.emotional_state: Peaceful. A smile she doesn't know she's making.

beats.text: The toaster could wait.
beats.tone: Final. Warm. Not sentimental — just true.

scenes.pacing_notes: Maximum slowness. Every gesture matters. The bag of corn should feel like the most important object in the world. End on the cabinet closing — no dialogue, no noise.
scenes.scene_thesis: Acceptance isn't dramatic. It's putting corn next to the cinnamon.
